source.title: Regulation of parkinsonian motor behaviours by optogenetic control of basal ganglia circuitry.

confidence_rationale: Landmark Nature paper providing first direct optogenetic proof of Go/NoGo pathway asymmetry: D1 direct pathway activation increases locomotion and approach; D2 indirect pathway activation elicits freezing, bradykinesia, and behavioral suppression. Directly demonstrates that the two channels are not redundant -- excitation of one does not substitute for the other. Bilateral D2 MSN activation produced a parkinsonian state (pure suppression / quiescence), precisely matching ARC-030's prediction that pure NoGo architecture produces behavioral flatness.
failure_signatures: If D1 and D2 pathways had symmetric or additive effects, bilateral D2 activation would not produce quiescence distinct from D1 activation.
If pure NoGo architecture were sufficient for goal-directed behavior, D2-activation animals would still initiate goal-directed locomotion -- they did not.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Bilateral excitation of indirect-pathway (D2) MSNs elicited a parkinsonian state distinguished by increased freezing, bradykinesia and decreased locomotor initiations. Activation of direct-pathway (D1) MSNs reduced freezing and increased locomotion.
mapping.ree_translation: In the REE three-loop BG model (ARC-030), each loop requires Go (D1/direct) sub-channels to drive approach and NoGo (D2/indirect) sub-channels to signal avoidance. This paper demonstrates that silencing Go while preserving NoGo (pharmacological or optogenetic indirect-pathway over-activation) produces behavioral quiescence -- the behavioral flatness predicted by ARC-030 when only the avoidance sub-channel is active.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Paper focuses on motor behavior, not explicit approach/avoidance learning in a cost-benefit task. The quiescence effect could reflect motor suppression rather than motivational flatness per se; however, decreased locomotor initiations maps directly onto a failure to approach rewards.
mapping.source_context: Study used Cre-dependent ChR2 viral expression in D1-Cre and D2-Cre transgenic mice; bilateral striatal stimulation; outcome measures: locomotor speed, freezing duration, locomotor initiations.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality (Nature, landmark optogenetic study). Mapping fidelity slightly reduced because the outcome is motor initiation, not reward-seeking per se, but the mechanistic homology is tight. Low transfer risk: the D1/D2 dissociation is a fundamental circuit property.
